coredata.dc:title: Colorimetric nanozyme sensor based on chain-like PtNi nanoparticles for nitrite ion detection in food samples
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Junli
Fang, Panpan
Li, Lei
Li, Li
coredata.dc:description: Here, we combine the peroxidase-like activity of chain-like PtNi nanoparticles (PtNi CNPs) and diazotization reaction to construct a dual-signal ratiometric colorimetric platform for nitrite ion detection. Compared to Pt NPs, PtNi CNPs exhibit better peroxidase-like activities with Michaelis constant (K m) of 0.15 mM and maximum reaction rate (V max) of 8.09 × 10−8 Ms.−1 towards 3, 3′, 5, 5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) and K m of 5.08 mM and V max of 16.07 × 10−8 Ms.−1 towards H2O2. After adding nitrite ion, diazotization reaction between oxidized TMB and nitrite ion occurs, resulting in an attenuated absorption peak signal at 652 nm and an enhanced absorption peak signal at 450 nm, accompanied by a color change (blue-yellow green). This dual-signal ratiometric (A652 nm/A450 nm) method shows a good linear relationship ranging from 20 to 350 μM and 0.3237 μM of detection limit. Importantly, the method shows great potential in detecting nitrite ion in real food samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Chain-like PtNi nanoparticles
Peroxidase-like activity
Colorimetric sensor
Nitrite ions

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The evaluation of peroxidase-like activities of PtNi CNPs
The procedure of colorimetric NO2 − assay
Peroxidase-like catalytic mechanism of PtNi CNPs
Steady-state kinetic assay of the peroxidase-like PtNi CNPs
Colorimetric detection of NO2 − based on PtNi CNPs' peroxidase-like activity
Feasibility of the sensing strategy to real samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) The schematic illustraction of synthesis process of PtNi CNPs. (B) TEM image and (C) HRTEM image of PtNi CNPs. High-resolution XPS spectra of (D) Pt 4f and (E) Ni 2p peaks of PtNi CNPs. (F) XRD patterns of pure Pt and PtNi CNPs. (G) STEM-EDS elemental mapping of PtNi CNPs.

(A) UV–Vis absorption spectra and the corresponding colors of PtNi+TMB + H2O2, PtNi+TMB, TMB + H2O2, and TMB solutions. (B) UV–Vis absorption spectra and the corresponding colors of PtNi+TMB + H2O2 and PtNi+TMB + H2O2-NO2 − solutions. (C) UV–Vis absorption spectra and the corresponding colors of PtNi+TMB + H2O2, PtNi+TMB + H2O2 + AA, PtNi+TMB + H2O2-NO2 −, PtNi+TMB + H2O2-NO2 −-AA, and TMB-NO2 − solutions. (D) Mechanism of the diazotization reaction between NO2 − and TMBox. (E) Mechanism of the redox reaction between diazotized TMBox and AA. PtNi CNP concentration: 0.0025 mg/mL, TMB concentration: 0.2 mM, H2O2 concentration: 40 mM, and AA concentration: 100 μM.

(A) The specific activities of PtNi CNPs and Pt NPs. (B) In situ FTIR spectra of H2O2 molecules on PtNi CNPs. The curves from top to bottom represent testing every 1 min for a total of 20 min. The error bars represent the standard deviations of three parallel measurements.

The variations in absorption signals at 652 nm against time in the presence of different concentrations of H2O2 for (A) PtNi CNPs and (B) Pt NPs. The variations in absorption signals at 652 nm against time in the presence of different concentrations of TMB for (E) PtNi CNPs and (F) Pt NPs. Michaelis-Menten curves with diverse concentrations of (C) H2O2 and (G) TMB for PtNi CNPs and Pt NPs. Double reciprocal Lineweaver-Burk plot of PtNi CNPs and Pt NPs towards (D) H2O2 and (H) TMB. (I) K m and (J) V max of PtNi CNPs, Pt NPs, and HRP for TMB and H2O2. (K) The comparison of K m and V max with TMB and H2O2 as the catalytic substrates of PtNi CNPs and Pt NPs nanozymes. The error bars represent the standard deviations of three parallel measurements. PtNi CNP concentration: 0.0025 mg/mL, TMB concentration: 0.2 mM, and H2O2 concentration: 40 mM.

(A) Schematic diagram of the constructed ratiometric dual-signal sensing platform for the visual detection of NO2 − using a smartphone. (B) The UV–Vis absorption spectra of the PtNi CNPs-TMB-H2O2 system with different concentration of NO2 −. (C) A652 nm/A450 nm as a function of NO2 − concentration (15–400 μM). (D) The linear calibration plot for NO2 − detection based on A652 nm/A450 nm signal. (E) The calculation of LOD (S/N = 3). (F) The variation curves of R, G, and B values of the PtNi CNPs-TMB-H2O2 solution color with different NO2 − concentration. (G) B values of RGB as a function of NO2 − concentration. (H) The linear correlations between the B value and the NO2 − concentration. (I) The calculation of LOD (S/N = 3). (J) The UV–Vis absorption spectra of the TMB solution with different concentration of NO2 −. (K) A436 nm as a function of NO2 − concentration. (L) The linear correlations between the A436 nm and the NO2 − concentration. (M) The calculation of LOD (S/N = 3). The error bars represent the standard deviations of three parallel measurements. PtNi CNP concentration: 0.0025 mg/mL, TMB concentration: 0.2 mM, and H2O2 concentration: 40 mM.

(A) Curve of the peroxidase-like activity stability of PtNi CNPs within 35 days. (B) Curve of the peroxidase-like activity stability of PtNi CNPs at different pH and temperature values. The error bars represent the standard deviations of three parallel measurements. PtNi CNP concentration: 0.0025 mg/mL, TMB concentration: 0.2 mM, and H2O2 concentration: 40 mM.

(A) The change in A652 nm/A450 nm after adding NO2 − and other interfering substances. The concentration of interfering substances is ten times that of NO2 − (10 mM). (B) The UV–Vis absorption spectra of the PtNi CNPs-TMB-H2O2 system in the presence of NO2 − (10 mM) and interfering substances (each at 100 mM). (C) CIE 1931 chromaticity diagrams from (D) the color photographs of the solutions in the presence of NO2 − and other interfering substances. PtNi CNP concentration: 0.0025 mg/mL, TMB concentration: 0.2 mM, and H2O2 concentration: 40 mM.
Schematic illustration of colorimetric nanozyme sensor based on peroxidase-like PtNi CNPs for nitrite ion detection.
Comparison of sensing performance of different nitrite sensors for nitrite.
Results of our PtNi CNPs nanozyme sensor for nitrite determination of in food samples.

row.entry: Detected by our method (meana ± SDb, μM)

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: meana: the average value of three measurements.

head.ce:title: Colorimetric nanozyme sensor based on chain-like PtNi nanoparticles for nitrite ion detection in food samples

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Materials Engineering, Xinxiang University, Xinxiang 453003, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Materials Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Materials Engineering, Xinxiang University, Xinxiang, 453003, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Henan Province Key Laboratory of New Opto-electronic Functional Materials, International Joint Laboratory of Henan Photoelectric Functional Materials, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Anyang Normal University, Anyang 455000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Henan Province Key Laboratory of New Opto-electronic Functional Materials, International Joint Laboratory of Henan Photoelectric Functional Materials
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Henan Province Key Laboratory of New Opto-electronic Functional Materials, International Joint Laboratory of Henan Photoelectric Functional Materials, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Anyang Normal University, Anyang 455000, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Here, we combine the peroxidase-like activity of chain-like PtNi nanoparticles (PtNi CNPs) and diazotization reaction to construct a dual-signal ratiometric colorimetric platform for nitrite ion detection. Compared to Pt NPs, PtNi CNPs exhibit better peroxidase-like activities with Michaelis constant (K m) of 0.15 mM and maximum reaction rate (V max) of 8.09 × 10−8 Ms.−1 towards 3, 3′, 5, 5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) and K m of 5.08 mM and V max of 16.07 × 10−8 Ms.−1 towards H2O2. After adding nitrite ion, diazotization reaction between oxidized TMB and nitrite ion occurs, resulting in an attenuated absorption peak signal at 652 nm and an enhanced absorption peak signal at 450 nm, accompanied by a color change (blue-yellow green). This dual-signal ratiometric (A652 nm/A450 nm) method shows a good linear relationship ranging from 20 to 350 μM and 0.3237 μM of detection limit. Importantly, the method shows great potential in detecting nitrite ion in real food samples.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Nitrite is widely present in the human environment and is a natural component of water and food (meat, vegetables, fruits, etc.). It has bactericidal, antibacterial, preservative, and antioxidant properties (Bayram & Ozturkcan, 2022; Guo et al., 2023; Shi et al., 2022). However, excessive or long-term intake of nitrite is more harmful to human health, including gastrointestinal tumors, blue baby syndrome, and stomach cancer (Kapil et al., 2020; Santharaman et al., 2017). In mild cases, cumulative intake of 0.3–0.5 mg of nitrite can cause food poisoning, while in severe cases, exceeding 3 g can lead to death (Lin et al., 2022). To protect food safety and human health, many countries have implemented regulations and standards for nitrite levels (Noori et al., 2022). For example, the United States Environmental Protection Agency and the World Health Organization (WHO) have set the maximum levels of nitrite contamination in drinking water at 1.0 and 3.0 mg per liter, respectively. Therefore, it is crucial to develop a portable, sensitive, and accurate method for determination of nitrite in food or drinking water.

To date, various approaches have been developed for nitrite detection, including chromatography (Doyle et al., 2000), high-performance liquid chromatography (He et al., 2011; Jobgen et al., 2007), chemiluminescence (Li et al., 2011; Lin et al., 2011; Shou et al., 2015), fluorescence (Jia et al., 2020; Büldt & Karst, 2023; Zhang et al., 2023), electrochemistry (Bagheri et al., 2017; Li et al., 2023; Tan et al., 2022), Raman spectroscopy (Zheng et al., 2018). These relatively large laboratory instruments usually have the characteristics of cumbersome operation, high cost, and long analysis time, which are not conducive to on-site analysis. Thanks to its low cost, simple operation, high sensitivity, and visible to the naked eye, colorimetric methods have received widespread attention (Liu, Gong, et al., 2023). Most remarkably, the colorimetric method based on the reaction of nitrite with other chemical reagents for diazotization is the simplest and most accurate method for detecting nitrite (Chen et al., 2020; Hong et al., 2022; Ortiz-Gomez et al., 2016; Viboonratanasri et al., 2018; Zhang et al., 2022; Zhao et al., 2014). However, the sensitivity of this method is relatively poor because of the low extinction coefficient of organic dyes.

Precious metal nanoparticles are widely used in colorimetric sensors due to their simplicity, practicality, biocompatibility, and good stability (Wang et al., 2019). Among precious metals, platinum-based alloys exhibit inherent excellent enzyme-like activity with high stability (Zhang et al., 2017). However, considering the scarcity, high cost, and low stability of platinum, this undoubtedly limits its widespread application (Mao et al., 2022). To solve this problem, doping transition metals (such as Ni, Co, Fe, etc.) into Pt is a good method to maintain its enzymatic catalytic activity and stability (Li et al., 2022; Ruan et al., 2022), which is attributed to the synergistic effect of the two metals in the alloy.

Inspired by this, we here report a simple one-step solvothermal method to synthesize chain-like PtNi nanoparticle (PtNi CNP) with peroxidase-like activities, and explore its application in colorimetric sensing of nitrite. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high resolution TEM characterizations show its morphology of the chain-like PtNi nanoparticles. The enzyme-catalyzed substrate color reaction proves that the PtNi CNPs possess excellent peroxidase-like properties, which exhibits a higher substrate affinity and 1-fold higher maximal reaction velocity than pure Pt NPs. After introducing the target nitrite, the diazotization reaction between nitrite and oxidized TMB (TMBox) alters the peroxidase-like catalytic activity of PtNi CNPs, resulting in a dual-signal UV–Vis absorption peak at 652 nm and 450 nm corresponding to TMBox and diazotized TMBox, respectively (Scheme 1 ). Thus, the nitrite concentration-dependent ratio change for the absorbance peaks at 652 nm and 450 nm (A652 nm/A450 nm) can be employed as the signal output, realizing the sensitive detection of nitrite with low detection limit (0.3237 μM).

3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB), H2O2 (30 %), nickel(II) acetylacetonate (Ni(acac)2), acetylacetone platinum (Pt(acac)2), cetyl trimethyl ammonium chloride (CTAC), oleylamine, and glucose were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Ethanol, cyclohexane, KCl, MgCl2, NaCl, Na2CO3, Na2SO4, NaNO3, and glutathione were obtained from Innochem. Ultrapure water (18.25 MΩ·cm) was purified by the Milli-Q system (France). All other materials used were analytical reagents.

UV–Vis absorption spectra were measured on a UV-2550 spectrophotometer (Shimazu, Japan). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were obtained from Tecnai G2 F20 (FEI, America). X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectra were measured using an X-ray diffractometer (D/MAX-RB, Japan). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was measured on K-Alpha 1063 (Thermo Fisher Scientific, British).

The PtNi CNPs were synthesized according to the literature (Luo et al., 2018). 20 mg of Pt (acac)2, 7 mg of Ni (acac)2, 32 mg of CTAC, and 90 mg of glucose were mixed. Then, 20 mL of oleylamine was added and sonicated for 30 min to form a homogeneous mixed solution. Next, the mixed solution was transferred to a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave. After sealing the autoclave, it was heated in an oven at 160 °C for 20 h and then cooled to room temperature. Subsequently, the black products were washed several times with a mixed solution of ethanol/cyclohexane (v: v, 9:1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The evaluation of peroxidase-like activities of PtNi CNPs
ce:section.ce:para: The peroxidase-like property was verified by the color variance of TMB. 25 μL of PtNi CNPs (0.1 mg/mL) was mixed with 100 μL of H2O2 (40 mM) and 100 μL of TMB (0.2 mM) in the 100 μL of HAc-NaAc buffer (0.1 M, pH 4.2). Then, the absorbance at 652 nm was recorded by a UV–Vis spectrophotometer, and the color photos of the solutions were recorded by digital camera. Finally, the Michaelis-Menten eq. (V = V max[S]/(K m + [S])) was employed to investigate the Michaelis constant (K m) and the maximum reaction rate (V max) of the PtNi CNPs.

Specific activity (SA) is defined as activity units of per milligram of nanozyme. A nanozyme activity unit (U) is defined as the amount of nanozyme that catalyzes 1 μmol of product per minute. The catalytic activity assay can be conducted with the common colorimetric substrate (TMB, ε 652 nm = 39,000 M−1 cm−1). The catalytic activity of the nanozyme is linearly proportional to its mass. This means that the SA value of the nanozyme can be determined by plotting the catalytic activity of the nanozyme against its weight and measuring the slope of the resulting line.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The procedure of colorimetric NO2 − assay
ce:section.ce:para: In a typical procedure, 25 μL of PtNi CNPs (0.0025 mg/mL), 100 μL of H2O2 (40 mM) and 100 μL of TMB (0.2 mM) were added into 675 μL of HAc-NaAc solution (100 mM, pH 4.2) at room temperature. After 23 min of incubation, different concentrations of NO2 − was added and a total volume of the solution was maintained at 1.0 mL. After a further 40 min of incubation at room temperature, the absorbance ratio of the solutions at 652 nm and 445 nm (A652 nm/A450 nm) was measured for nitrite quantification. For the smartphone-assisted detection of NO2 −, the peroxidase-like PtNi CNPs-TMB-H2O2 system in the presence of NO2 − were added into a 96-well plate. Afterwards, Huawei smartphone captured digital images through the “Color Grab” application and convert them into R, G, and B parameters respectively.

To explore the applicability of the sensing strategy, the real samples (pickled vegetables, pickled mustard tuber, and pickled cucumber) were used. The pickled vegetables, the pickled cucumber, and the pickled mustard tuber samples were purchased from the supermarket. The pickled food samples were processed as follows: accurately weigh 1.0 g of pickled food sample and place it in 10 mL centrifuge tube. Then, add 8 mL of deionized water to it and sonicate the mixed solution for 30 min. After separating nitrite, let the water stand for 30 min. Take 2 mL of supernatant, add 100 μL of 1 mM newly prepared NaF solution and 30 mg of activated carbon powder. NaF can remove the interference of Fe3+ in the sample, while activated carbon powder can absorb colored substances, metal ions, and organic compounds. After leaving the solution for 3 h, centrifuge the solution at 10000 rpm for 10 min. Finally, remove the supernatant and filter it through a 0.22 μm membrane.

Then, the above pickled food samples were diluted with the HAc-NaAc buffer solution. Next, the NO2 − solutions at diverse concentrations were spiked into the pretreated samples. The subsequent detection process was similar to that for NO2 − detection in HAc-NaAc buffer.

The PtNi CNPs were synthesized via simple one-step solvothermal method (Fig. 1A). A series of means such as TEM, XRD, and XPS, and EDS mapping were employed to characterize the as-prepared PtNi CNPs. Fig. 1B exhibits the typical TEM image of the chain-like PtNi nanoparticles with a length of hundreds of nanometers. HRTEM image (Fig. 1C) shows lattice fringes of 0.219 nm and 0.221 nm of PtNi CNPs, corresponding to (111) facets of Pt and PtNi alloys, respectively, indicating the successful synthesis of PtNi CNPs. The atomic ratio of elemental Pt to Ni was approximately 3.26:1 by Inductively Coupled Plasma Mass Spectrometry (ICP-MS). XPS measurements were measured to characterize the surface characteristics of PtNi CNPs. As shown in Fig. 1D,E, high-resolution XPS spectra show that Pt 4f and Ni 2p peaks could be clearly observed and Pt on the PtNi CNPs surface was in the form of Pt(0) (Xi et al., 2021). The XRD pattern of PtNi CNPs was used to analyze structural changes after doping Ni into Pt NPs. As shown in Fig. 1F, the diffraction peaks located at 40°, 46°, 67°, 81°, and 86° correspond to the (111), (200), (220), (311), and (222) planes of the Pt standard card (JCPDS No. 04–0802), respectively, exhibiting a typical face-centered cubic (fcc) structure. Compared with pure Pt, the diffraction peaks of Pt Ni CNPs have a slight shift towards higher Bragg angles, which is because the introduction of metallic Ni results in a decrease in the lattice spacing of Pt during alloying. Scanning transmission electron microscopy (STEM)-EDS elemental mapping (Fig. 1) shows the homogeneous distribution of Ni and Pt elements throughout the zone, proving the structure of PtNi alloy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Peroxidase-like catalytic mechanism of PtNi CNPs
ce:section.ce:para: In order to explore the peroxidase-like catalytic mechanism of the PtNi CNPs, TMB was used as a chromogenic substrate. Impressively, under naked eye observation, the TMB solution color changes from colorless to blue only in the presence of both PtNi CNPs and H2O2, and a significant absorption peak appears at 652 nm. In contrast, TMB, TMB + H2O2, and PtNi CNPs+TMB solutions were colorless in color, and the absorption peaks at 652 nm were negligible (Fig. 2A). Obviously, PtNi CNPs exhibit excellent peroxidase-like activity in the catalytic oxidation of TMB, rather than oxidase-like activity. When nitrite was added to the TMB + H2O2 + PtNi CNPs solution in the acidic environment, the color of the solution changed from blue to green. Correspondingly, the absorption signal at 652 nm decreased, while the absorption signal at 445 nm was dramatically increased (Fig. 2B). The above phenomenon was attributed to the diazotization interaction between TMBox and NO2 −: Due to the presence of aromatic primary amine groups in TMBox, it can undergo diazotization reaction with NO2 − in acidic environment, resulting in the consumption of blue TMBox and the generation of diazotized TMBox substances (Fig. 2D). To further prove the diazotization interaction occurs between TMBox and NO2 −, ascorbic acid (AA) was selected as a reducing agent and added to two systems (TMB + H2O2 + PtNi CNPs and NO2 −-TMB + H2O2 + PtNi CNPs). As shown in Fig. 2C, when AA was added into the TMB + H2O2 + PtNi CNPs solution, the blue TMBox changed to colorless TMB. While the addition of AA to NO2 −-TMB + H2O2 + PtNi CNPs system made the color change from green to yellow, with the decrease of the absorption peak at 445 nm and disappearance of the absorption peak at 652 nm (Fig. 2E). AA can reduce the semi-quinone-imine structure in diazotized TMBox to the aromatic hydrocarbon amino group structure structure, but it cannot reduce the diazo structure. Thus, the residual absorbance peak at 445 nm may be a product of diazotized TMB.

The specific activities (SAs) of PtNi CNPs and Pt NPs nanozymes were quantitatively compared by measuring the absorbance of TMBox at 652 nm. As shown in Fig. 3A, excitingly, the SA of PtNi CNPs is 29.8 U/mg, 9.14-fold higher than that Pt NPs (3.26 U/mg), illustrating that PtNi CNPs have higher peroxidase-like catalytic activity. Furthermore, in situ Fourier translation infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was conducted to explore adsorbed intermediates on PtNi CNPs during the peroxidase-like catalytic process. As depicted in Fig. 3B, after H2O2 activation, two different signals were observed at 1339 cm−1 and 1047 cm−1, corresponding to the OH stretching modes of H2O2 and OH of water adsorbed on PtNi CNPs. Based on the above results, H2O2 may decompose into adsorbed *OH during the catalytic process, serving as an intermediate in the catalytic reaction. It is worth noting that the peak intensity at 1047 cm−1 is higher than that at 1339 cm−1, indicating an improved catalytic activity of PtNi CNPs in decomposing H2O2 into ·OH and adsorbing it onto PtNi CNPs' surface. In addition, the peak at 1278 cm−1 comes from the stretching vibration of OO in H2O2, which gradually decreases due to partial dissociation of H2O2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Steady-state kinetic assay of the peroxidase-like PtNi CNPs
ce:section.ce:para: In order to further evaluate peroxidase-like catalytic activities of PtNi CNPs, the steady-state kinetics assay was performed by changing TMB or H2O2 concentration. Meanwhile, the steady-state kinetic parameters of Pt NPs were also measured and compared with those of PtNi CNPs. By measuring the variations in absorption signals at 652 nm against time in the presence of different concentrations of H2O2 or TMB (fixed TMB or H2O2 concentration) (Fig. 4A,B,E,F), the kinetics parameters, such as Michaelis constant (K m) and maximum reaction rate (V max), can be calculated via the Lineweaver-Burk equation (Fig. 4D,H). As shown in Fig. 4(C,G), the catalytic reactions of the TMB and H2O2 substrates involved in PtNi CNPs and Pt NPs nanozymes both obeyed well to the standard Michaelis-Menten model, showing the K m of 0.15 mM and V max of 8.09 × 10−8 M s−1 with TMB as the catalytic substrate and K m of 5.08 mM and V max of 16.07 × 10−8 M s−1 with H2O2 as the catalytic substrate of PtNi CNP nanozyme. Compared with Pt NPs and peroxidase horseradish peroxidase (HRP), as shown in Fig. 4(I,J,K), the PtNi CNP nanozyme exhibited better affinity towards TMB substrate and higher reaction rate towards H2O2 substrate. Meanwhile, the kinetics parameters of PtNi CNPs were better than most other peroxidases (Table S1). These scenarios evidenced the superior peroxidase-like catalytic activities of PtNi CNPs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Colorimetric detection of NO2 − based on PtNi CNPs' peroxidase-like activity
ce:section.ce:para: In order to acquire better conditions for NO2 − detection, the conditions were firstly optimized including solution pH, H2O2 concentration, PtNi CNPs content, the reaction time between TMB and H2O2, and the reaction time between TMBox and NO2 −. As is well known, due to the presence of aromatic primary amines in TMBox, diazotization reaction occurs between TMBox and NO2 − in acidic environment. Therefore, we selected HAc-NaAc buffer solutions with different pH values to determine the optimal pH value for the system. As shown in Fig. S1, when the pH was 4.2, the peroxidase-like catalytic activity reached its maximum. For the optimization of H2O2 concentration, the catalytic activity of the PtNi CNPs increases by changing the H2O2 concentration from 20 to 40 mM and then decreases beyond 40 mM (Fig. S2). Clearly, 40 mM was chosen as the optimal H2O2 concentration. Fig. S3 shows that as the content of PtNi CNPs increased from 0.0005 mg/mL to 0.0030 mg/mL, the peroxidase-like activity of PtNi CNPs first increased and then decreased, achieving the maximum value when the content was 0.0025 mg/mL. The reaction time between TMB and H2O2 was optimized. As shown in Fig. S4, when the reaction time reached 23 min, the absorbance at 652 nm reached its maximum value. Therefore, the optimal reaction time between TMB and H2O2 was 23 min. Finally, the effect of the reaction time of TMBox and NO2 − on the catalytic activities was investigated. Fig. S5 shows that as the reaction time passed, the A652 nm/A450 nm gradually decreased and reached a plateau at 40 min. Thus, 40 min was selected as the optimal reaction time between TMBox and NO2 −. Under the optimized conditions, colorimetric sensing of NO2 − was conduct (Fig. 5A). As shown in Fig. 5B, due to the diazotization reaction, as the concentration of NO2 − increases, the absorbance of the system gradually increased at 450 nm, while conversely, the absorbance at 652 gradually decreased. As the NO2 − level increased, A652/A450, as the vertical axis, gradually decreased. When the concentration of NO2 − reached 400 μM, the A652/A450 signal tended to stabilize (Fig. 5C). Meanwhile, the color of the solution changed from blue to green and then to yellow green (inset of Fig. 5C). A good linear relationship between the A652/A450 and the NO2 − concentration over the range of 15–400 μM (Fig. 5D) was obtained, with a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.3237 μM (S/N = 3) (Fig. 5E). In addition, we have also adopted a more direct visual strategy to detect NO2 −. Fig. 5F shows the variation curves of red (R), green (G), and blue (B) values of the solution color with NO2 − concentration. Fig. 5G shows that the B value of RGB in the reaction system decreases with increasing nitrite levels. When reaching 350 μM, the signal tended to flatten out. With the increase of the nitrite concentration, the color of the solution changed from blue to yellow green (inset of Fig. 5G). The B value had a good linear relationship with the concentration of nitrite in the range of 30–350 μM (Fig. 5H), with a LOD of 6.202 μM (Fig. 5I). Notably, NO2 − can directly react with TMB substrate under acidic conditions without the need for a catalyst, forming a clear absorption peak at 436 nm (Fig. 5J) and changing color from colorless to yellow (Fig. 5K). Due to the oxidation-reduction and diazotization reactions between TMB and nitrite, colorimetric signals are generated at 652 nm and 436 nm. Through this method, NO2 − can also be directly detected. However, the peak intensity at 652 nm varied irregularly. Therefore, we used the absorption intensity at 436 nm to quantify the concentration of nitrite ions. With the increasing of NO2 − concentration, the absorption peak at 436 nm was significantly enhanced as can be seen from the absorbance in Fig. 5J,K. There was a good linear relationship between the absorbance at 436 nm and NO2 − concentration (30–300 μM) (Fig. 5L) with a LOD of 0.6594 μM (Fig. 5M). Comparing the results from the three methods mentioned above, it can be concluded that the A652 nm/A450 nm detection showed higher sensitivity than RGB mode and A436 nm method. The sensing performance (linear range and detection limit) for NO2 −, as compared with that of other NO2 − sensors (Table 1 ), was comparative or even better. The reason for this was as follows: First, the chain-like nanostructure possessed abundant defects and high index crystal planes, which have been proven to be highly active catalytic sites; Second, PtNi alloy can better exert the synergistic catalytic effect of the two metals. In addition to the lower detection limit and wider linear range of the ratio colorimetric sensors for nitrite detection, more importantly, the ratio colorimetric method have more advantages, such as overcoming single signal instability, good selectivity, and avoiding environmental influences. In addition, the catalytic stability of PtNi CNPs was investigated. As shown in Fig. 6A, after 35 days, the peroxidase-like activity still kept above 93.9 %. Notably, PtNi CNPs maintained excellent peroxidase-like activity over a wide range of pH (3.6–10) and temperature (20–90 °C) (Fig. 6B).

In order to verify the selectivity of the PtNi CNPs nanozyme for the NO2 − detection, control experiments were performed with K+, Mg2+, Na+, Cl−, CO3 2−, SO4 2−, NO3 −, glucose, and glutathione (GSH) as potential interfering substances. The concentration of interfering substances (100 mM) was ten times that of nitrite ion (10 mM). In this event, the A652 nm/A450 nm pronouncedly decreased only in the presence of NO2 −, which was distinct from other interfering species (Fig. 7A,B). Obviously, from a color perspective, only the NO2 − solution turned yellow, while other solutions containing interfering substances remained blue like the blank solution (Fig. 7C,D), demonstrating the acceptable selectivity of the present colorimetric strategy for NO2 − detection. The excellent selectivity was ascribed to the diazotized reaction between NO2 − and TMBox.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Feasibility of the sensing strategy to real samples
ce:section.ce:para: In order to explore the application performance of this nanozyme sensor on actual samples, we tried to determine NO2 − in food such as pickled vegetables, pickled cucumber, and pickled mustard tuber. To avoid any interference from the sample matrix, the standard addition method is carried out by adding a known concentration of NO2 − to each sample. Before adding nitrite ions, the original actual samples were measured by both our method and commercial detection kit based on Griess reaction (GB 5009.33–2016). As shown in Table 2 , the recovery rates were located from 94.16 % to 103.70 % with the relative standard deviation (RSD) below 3.52 % for NO2 − detection. The results obtained by our nanozyme sensor coincided with that obtained by the commercial kit detection, which confirmed the PtNi CNPs nanozyme-based sensing strategy for NO2 − determination in food samples with high reliability.

In summary, we developed a peroxidase-like PtNi CNPs nanozyme-based colorimetric method for NO2 − determination. Compared to RGB (B) and A436 nm signal-based colorimetric methods, A652 nm/A450 nm-based method presented superior sensing performance with a LOD down to 0.3237 μM due to the dual signal (A652 nm and A450 nm) generated by the system. Besides, the sensing strategy showed excellent selectivity towards NO2 − because of PtNi CNPs' outstanding peroxidase-like catalytic activities and diazotization reaction. Notably, the practicability of the colorimetric platform has been verified by detection of NO2 − in food samples.
Junli Wang: Writing – review & editing, Data curation, Conceptualization. Panpan Fang: Methodology, Data curation. Lei Li: Resources, Methodology. Li Li: Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21801215) and Scientific Research Starting Foundation of Xinxiang University for Doctors (1366020123).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2025.144715.
